decisive_findings: Side A responded more fully to critique by downgrading its strongest unsupported prioritization claim into a testable hypothesis and adding explicit root-cause validation gates.
Side A produced a more decision-useful PRD because it defined concrete proceed/kill conditions, readiness gates, and a bounded next artifact tied to evidence collection.

Side B improved internal consistency, but its final artifact remained lighter on validation mechanics and continued to rely on thin evidence support anchored mainly to the prompt rather than a stronger evidentiary frame.
rationale: Side A is the stronger final artifact because it not only preserved scope discipline but also converted the main critique into a materially better decision framework: the revised PRD now treats handoff leverage as a hypothesis, adds a comparative root-cause check, and defines explicit pass/fail gates before build commitment. Side B also improved meaningfully by reframing ownership and notifications as validation-gated decisions, but it remains more generic and less operationally decisive. Neither side has strong external evidence, so evidence-discipline scores stay moderate, but Side A wins on responsiveness to critique, internal coherence after revision, and practical usefulness for deciding whether to proceed.
